EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow eastward-moving surface boundary in association with a broad upper level trough over the Ohio Valley progressed across the Blue Ridge during the afternoon and evening. Thunderstorms trained northward along the boundary, repeatedly over the same area in Culpeper County.  Torrential rains produced flash flooding in portions of the county.  A maximum measured rainfall total of 7.9 inches was reported near Winston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous road closures were reported in Culpeper County.  A rain gage in Culpeper measured 4.58 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer southerly flow advected a moist, tropical airmass into the Mid-Atlantic.  As low pressure approached from the west, periods of heavy rain and thunderstorms moved across the central Virginia Foothills.  As grounds became saturated, flood and flash flooding resulted in some areas.  A spotter in Albemarle County measured 3.15 inches of rain during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crabtree Falls Highway near Cub Creek Road was closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moving through the Mid Mississippi and Lower Ohio Valley produced strong convection across Kentucky and Northern Middle Tennessee early in the day. Severe convection continued to form well into the afternoon in the vicinity of a west through east outflow boundary in place across extreme Southwest Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A return flow around high pressure pumped in plenty of warm and moist air over the region. Colder air aloft associated with an upper-level trough combined with the warm and moist air at the surface to develop moderate amounts of instability. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small to medium sized limbs were damaged by thunderstorm winds.

The deep moisture combined with a mid-level trough that was associated with the cold front to produce moderate amounts of instability. Lift from the cold front along with moderate instability triggered showers and thunderstorms across the area.||Thunderstorms were able to mix down strong winds from aloft causing damaging wind gusts.

Convection became organized along the boundary transitioning into an mesoscale convective system which moved across Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down on Oakwood Street and Pine Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moving through the Mid Mississippi and Lower Ohio Valley produced strong convection across Kentucky and Northern Middle Tennessee early in the day. The convection organized into a Mesoscale Convective System as it moved into the unstable atmosphere across Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee. Severe convection continued to form well into the afternoon in the vicinity of a west through east outflow boundary in place across extreme Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere destabilized during the late morning into afternoon period as a cold front progressed into the Southern Appalachian region. Large hail was the primary mode of severe weather as a pocket of cold air aloft moved across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell along interstate 81.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere destabilized during the late morning into afternoon period as a cold front progressed into the Southern Appalachian region. Large hail was the primary mode of severe weather as a pocket of cold air aloft moved across the forecast area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving warm front lifted northward through northern Virginia during the afternoon and evening. Over 24 hours of intermittent rain had created saturated grounds across the area and even a report of morning flooding.  Thus, when thunderstorms fired later in the day as instability and wind fields strengthened, high rainfall rates associated with them lead to flash flooding in several locations in the western suburbs of Washington DC, and flooding continued for a while after rains ended.  The highest rainfall amount received was 4.11 inches in Fairfax County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front passed through central and northern Virginia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 13th. A potent upper-level trough approached the area during the same time causing strong winds aloft. Thunderstorms that developed behind the front were able to produce damaging wind gusts due to the strong winds aloft. Winds rapidly changed in both direction and speed with height causing some of the stronger thunderstorms to produce tornadoes near the warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least four large trees were down and partially blocking the roadway between Hunter Mill Road and just north of the intersection of Hunter Mill Road and Vale Road.

Over 24 hours of intermittent rain had created saturated grounds across the area and even a report of morning flooding.  Thus, when thunderstorms fired later in the day as instability and wind fields strengthened, high rainfall rates associated with them lead to flash flooding in several locations in the western suburbs of Washington DC, and flooding continued for a while after rains ended.  The highest rainfall amount received was 4.11 inches in Fairfax County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hunter Mill Road closed south of the intersection of Hunter Mill Road and Cedar Pond Road.  Water was flowing across the roadway with depth of at least 6 inches.  A nearby rain gauge tallied 1.53 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were reported between 30 and 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant hydrologic event of the month began around the 28th as an upper level low closed off over the lower Mississippi Valley and lifted slowly northeast over the next 24 to 36 hours. Rainfall became widespread and heavy in the mountains of North Carolina in the afternoon hours of the 28th and gradually spread northeast into Virginia.  The prolonged upslope event produced storm total rainfall ranging from 2 to 4 inches in a 12 hour period. Rainfall rates were generally around 0.5 inches per hour during the most intense portion of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Masons Creek flooded part of Bendemeer Road off of Bradshaw Road (Route 622). The Mason's Creek IFLOWS gage (MSCV2) crested at 6.4 feet around 6 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant hydrologic event of the month began around the 28th as an upper level low closed off over the lower Mississippi Valley and lifted slowly northeast over the next 24 to 36 hours. The prolonged upslope event produced storm total rainfall ranging from 2 to 4 inches in a 12 hour period. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roanoke River flooded a portion of West Main and West River Street. The Roanoke River at Glenvar (GNVV2) located upstream crested at 10.01 feet (Flood Stage - 9') while the downstream gage at Roanoke (RONV2) crested at 7.81 feet a few hours after this (Flood Stage - 10').

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with a weak cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Storm Survey determined the damage was consistent with straight line winds estimated between 80 and 90 mph. The winds did damage in a small area adjacent to the Bermuda Crossroads marketplace shopping center. Two buildings suffered damage. One building had part of the roof peeled off allowing water damage to occur inside the building. The second building, a vacant masonry warehouse structure, suffered much more damage as winds blew down hanging garage doors and then blew out the back wall of the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Canadian high pressure build over the Potomac Highlands of Virginia during the early morning hours of the 5th. Mainly clear skies along with light winds allowed temperatures to drop below freezing during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were estimated to be in the upper 20s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent onshore existed between high pressure over New England and low pressure over the Midwest. The onshore flow caused tidal anomalies to increase over the Tidal Potomac River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water levels were above thresholds for moderate coastal flooding at Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low remained over the region on the 18th. Moderate instability developed due to the unusual amount of cold air aloft. Showers and thunderstorms developed due to the instability. Some thunderstorms did produce large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported on Hardwood Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low remained over the region on the 18th. Moderate instability developed due to the unusual amount of cold air aloft. Showers and thunderstorms developed due to the instability. Some thunderstorms did produce large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up the size of ping pong balls was reported along Pinewood Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the region on the evening of the 21st. High pressure to the north allowed for plenty of cold air to move into the region during the nighttime hours of the 21st. A wave of low pressure was moving along the front during this time causing precipitation over the area. Precipitation initially started out as rain before changing over to ice and snow as colder air worked its way in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice accumulation from freezing rain was estimated across the county, especially at high elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front passed through the region on the 21st. Limited instability developed behind the front due to a southwest flow. An upper-level disturbance tracked through the area during this time to combine with the instability, bringing a few showers and thunderstorms. A few thunderstorms did produce damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was measured near Monterey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the area on the 10th. Plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean were drawn into the region ahead of the front. A warm and moist southerly flow ahead of the front caused limited instability to develop. Strong lift from the front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms.  ||Winds just a few thousand feet above the surface were quite strong. Some thunderstorms were able to transport these winds down to the surface during the afternoon and evening hours of the 10th. There was also enough rotation at the lower levels of the atmosphere for isolated tornadoes in the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were down and a well constructed Barn was collapsed near the intersection of Route 28 and Old Carolina Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorm in advance of a trough of low pressure produced damaging winds across portions of eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system over the Tennessee Valley tracked across the southern Appalachians helping to pull a cold front across the area during the afternoon. A line of storms developed along the front over the mountains and the line strengthened during the evening as it moved into somewhat more unstable air over the piedmont. Wind shear was sufficient to provide some organization to the embedded storm elements and a brief EF1 tornado was confirmed by an NWS storm survey team north of Danville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down or had their tops snapped off. In addition, several powerlines were blown down, a trampoline and a metal sheet shed were blown into a field, and a wooden play set was snapped in two. The underpinning of a trailer was also blown loose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Ohio Valley and low pressure off the New England Coast brought gusty winds to central Virginia. The strongest winds were across the ridge tops along the Blue Ridge Mountains and Potomac Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was reported at Wintergreen.

Minor tree damage continued across US Route 50 near the International Town and Country Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of low pressure over the Ohio Valley produced several days of intermittent showers and thunderstorms across the mid-Atlantic leading up to May 16th. On the afternoon and evening of May 16, training thunderstorms over saturated produced in excess of 2 inches of rain across portions of Prince William County and adjacent independent cities. This prompted flash flooding over the southwestern suburbs of the Washington metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high water rescue was underway near the intersection of Lucasville Road and Hidden Spring Drive. Water was 3 feet deep. A rain gage nearby measured 1.90 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable atmospheric conditions resulted in a deadly tornado outbreak across southwest Virginia on the 27th. The tornadoes produced from light to heavy damage to many homes and businesses across southeast Virginia. The thunderstorms produced 4 tornadoes across the area along with 4 deaths and 50 injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Green Cove Tornado.  A NWS Storm Survey reported an EF1 tornado touched down near Green Cove on the Virginia Creeper Trail. It had a path width of 200 yards and a path length of 0.5 miles. The max wind of 90 mph downed numerous trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the Mid-Atlantic Coast spreading rain across northern and central Virginia on the 6th. As the low passed by to the east, colder air was drawn into the system causing rain to change to snow during the afternoon and evening hours over portions of the Potomac Highlands and Shenandoah Valley. Snow ended early on the 7th. The heaviest snow amounts were over the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 4.5 inches near Wild Acres and 1.5 inches near Hayfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable atmospheric conditions resulted in a deadly tornado outbreak across southwest Virginia on the 27th. The tornadoes produced from light to heavy damage to many homes and businesses across southeast Virginia. The thunderstorms produced 4 tornadoes across the area along with 4 deaths and 50 injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glade Spring Tornado.  An NWS storm survey reported an EF3 tornado formed south of Abingdon and tracked northeast to the Glade Spring area then moved into Smyth County Virginia. The 140 mph wind speed associated with the tornado, destroyed a small church as well as producing extensive damage to many residences and businesses. The tornado crossed I-81 at the Glade Spring exit (Exit 29) and caused extensive damage to a large truck stop as well as flipping trucks on the property.  One truck turned on its side and smashed into the corner of the restaurant.  ||Three people died and 50 were injured by the tornado.  A 59 year old man and a 67 year old women died when the tornado destroyed their mobile homes.  In addition, a 71 year old man died in his home.  An indirect death occurred when a 60-year-old woman, driving a car rear-ended a stationary tractor trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant hydrologic event of the month began around the 28th as an upper level low closed off over the lower Mississippi Valley and lifted slowly northeast over the next 24 to 36 hours. Rainfall became widespread and heavy in the mountains of North Carolina in the afternoon hours of the 28th and gradually spread northeast into Virginia.  The prolonged upslope event produced storm total rainfall ranging from 2 to 4 inches in a 12 hour period. Rainfall rates were generally around 0.5 inches per hour during the most intense portion of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Glendale Road just east of Bee Line Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front passed through on the evening of the 18th. Strong westerly winds combined with low relative humidity and low fuel moisture to cause the development of wildfires in central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was reported about 10 miles west of Harrisonburg. The size of the fire was reported to be at 3,479 acres. Peake Mountain Road was closed along with Second Mountain Road, Long Run Road, and Kephart Run road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure quickly passed by to the south during the morning hours of the 27th. High pressure to the north supplied enough cold air to cause precipitation associated with the low to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were estimated to be around 2.0 inches across most of eastern Highland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the region on the evening of the 21st. High pressure to the north allowed for plenty of cold air to move into the region during the nighttime hours of the 21st. A wave of low pressure was moving along the front during this time causing precipitation over the area. Precipitation initially started out as rain before changing over to ice and snow as colder air worked its way in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice accumulation from freezing rain was reported near Westover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system over the Tennessee Valley tracked across the southern Appalachians helping to pull a cold front across the area during the afternoon. A line of storms developed along the front over the mountains and the line strengthened during the evening as it moved into somewhat more unstable air over the piedmont. Wind shear was sufficient to provide some organization to the embedded storm elements and a brief EF1 tornado was confirmed by an NWS storm survey team north of Danville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds on Rodgers Chapel Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was downed and blocking the intersection of Studley Road and Shady Grove Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the area on the 10th. Plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean were drawn into the region ahead of the front. A warm and moist southerly flow ahead of the front caused limited instability to develop. Strong lift from the front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms.  ||Winds just a few thousand feet above the surface were quite strong. Some thunderstorms were able to transport these winds down to the surface during the afternoon and evening hours of the 10th. There was also enough rotation at the lower levels of the atmosphere for isolated tornadoes in the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down at Courthouse and Blockhouse Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of upper level low pressure system over the Ohio Valley provided plenty of lift to combine with an usually strong and moist southeasterly low-level flow to produce bands of moderate to heavy showers across the mid-Atlantic. With saturated ground in place and the training nature of the widespread showers, flood and flash flooding was the result across portions of northern Virginia. Rainfall amounts of over 3 inches were common and included a measurement of 4.48 inches from Earlysville, VA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morgan Ford Road was closed due to a flash flood on Manassas Run.

Tree damage was reported at several points in the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds behind the cold front were very strong nearly all day with sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph and measured gusts exceeding 50 mph. There were several reports of trees being blown down, partly due to wet soils after recently ended rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot Springs (KHSP) AWOS gusted to 55 mph at 1:35 PM and had frequent gusts over 40 mph throughout the day. Ther were two reports of trees down due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of low pressure over the Ohio Valley produced several days of intermittent showers and thunderstorms across the mid-Atlantic leading up to May 16th. On the afternoon and evening of May 16, training thunderstorms over saturated produced in excess of 2 inches of rain across portions of Prince William County and adjacent independent cities. This prompted flash flooding over the southwestern suburbs of the Washington metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fleetwood Drive was closed near Parkgate Drive due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north from Georgia to Illinois. Abundant moisture and energy in advance of it streamed north across the region bringing abundant showers, and a few thunderstorms, to the area. Damaging winds accompanied one of the storms and brought some trees down in Huddleston in Bedford County. Very moist soil conditions may also have contributed to the tree falling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew trees down south of Huddleston along Route 630. Very moist soil conditions may also have contributed to the falling trees. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of snow showers were associated with the arrival of colder air from late Thursday evening the 6th into Saturday evening the 8th.  The snow showers were the heaviest overnight Friday night, which was late on the 7th into the early hours of the 8th.  The old December snow pack had mostly melted by New Years Day.  This 48 hour episode brought new snow accumulations of 3 to 7 inches.
Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with a trough of low pressure produced damaging winds across portions of central and eastern Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front passed through the region on the 21st. Limited instability developed behind the front due to a southwest flow. An upper-level disturbance tracked through the area during this time to combine with the instability, bringing a few showers and thunderstorms. A few thunderstorms did produce damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarters in size was reported in McDowell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Buchanan and Dickenson Counties were on the northern fringe of the deeper moisture associated with a southern storm track.   Snow accumulated only 2 to 4 inches.   However, air temperatures were well below freezing.  Road surfaces were also cold.  Untreated surfaces were quickly coated, causing hazardous travel during the evening of the 9th.

Colder air blew in during the overnight period of the 11th into the 12th.  Snow showers continued into the early hours of the 13th.  Snow accumulations were mostly 2 to 6 inches.  Clintwood snow depth increased from 4 inches prior to the event to 9 inches.  Nora had their snow depth increase from 3 inches to 8 inches.  However, Grundy only saw their snow depth increase from 1 inch to 3 inches.

A strong upper level low pressure spinning over the eastern Great Lakes pushed several disturbances across southwest Virginia. These disturbances combined with persistent northwest flow led to accumulating snow showers across the higher elevations from the 7th into the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Showers fell heaviest on the 8th across the county. Only 2 to 4 inches fell near Independence. However higher amounts near 6 inches were observed across the higher elevations in the western portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the Mid-Atlantic Coast on the 17th before moving toward New England on the 18th. Precipitation overspread the area on the night of the 17th ahead of the low. Warmer air was drawn into the system, but cold high pressure over New England kept enough low-level cold air in place for a prolonged period of frozen precipitation into the early morning hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation from freezing rain was measured around one tenth of an inch at Leesburg. Snow accumulations averaged around an inch across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper-level high pressure built over the region during the 22nd. Surface high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean caused moist air to move into the region from the south. Strong subsidence underneath the upper-level high caused extremely hot conditions with air temperatures over 100 degrees. The combination of the heat and high humidity caused heat indices to soar over 115 degrees. The worst conditions were east of the Blue Ridge Mountains where the humidity was highest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices in excess of 115 degrees were estimated due to observations nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow around high pressure off the east coast produced hot and humid conditions. The hot and humid conditions combined with colder air aloft associated with an upper-level trough to produce moderate amounts of instability. Some thunderstorms that developed produced damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported near Browntown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the area during the 3rd. A southerly flow ahead of the front caused enough warm and moist air for moderate instability to develop. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs were down that were three inches and larger in diameter. The damage occurred along Route 17 and 155 on Winchester Road.

The combination of the heat and high humidity caused heat indices to soar over 115 degrees. The worst conditions were east of the Blue Ridge Mountains where the humidity was highest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices were estimated to be around 110 degrees based on observations nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance passed through the area on the 11th. The atmosphere was very unstable due to a hot and humid air mass in place. The combination of forcing from the upper-level disturbance along with high amounts of instability led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least seven large trees about one foot in diameter or more were down near the Fairfax County Parkway and West Ox Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pressure trough triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms across central Virginia. Moderate instability ahead of the pressure trough caused thunderstorms to produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blocking Zachary Taylor Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Depression Lee interacted with a slow-moving cold front to produce widespread showers across the area. High rainfall amounts associated with some of this activity prompted flooding and flash flooding in a few locations. Rainfall totals were a high as 8.90 inches in Nelson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and subsequent flooding caused sink hole on Avon Street.  The street was closed for prolonged period.  A spotter in Charlottesville reported 2.80 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge was centered over the southern Plains while an upper-level low was located over central Canada. A northwest flow between these two systems caused colder air aloft to move overhead. Warm and moist air at the surface combined with the colder air aloft to produce moderate amounts of instability. A pressure trough at the surface caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Moderate instability caused some thunderstorms to produce damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees up to ten inches in diameter were snapped thirty to forty feet up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved into the far western sections of the area producing heavy rainfall of 1.5 to over 2 inches in parts of the Tennessee River basin. 24-hour rain gage totals ending at 7AM on March 1st were highest across the Mountain Empire and included 2.24��� at Fairwood IFLOWS (FWDV2), 2.08��� at Springville IFLOWS (SPIV2), 1.96��� at both Jones Knob IFLOWS (JNKV2) and Grayson Highlands IFLOWS (GYHV2), and 1.90��� at Troutdale COOP (TROV2). There were sharp rises on several rivers and streams. The Clinch River at Richlands (RLRV2) in western Tazewell County hit 9.60 feet, just below the minor flood stage of 10 feet. This was the highest level on this gage since May 25, 2004 (10.37���). Both the North and South Forks of the Holston River in Smyth County rose to near action stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported at several locations with water onto roadways due to the heavy rains and poor drainage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of over an inch were common late on Saturday the 5th.  A strong cold front came through during the predawn hours of Sunday the 6th.  A few hours in the wake of the surface front, the rain changed to wet snow.  Snow accumulations were highly dependent on elevations.  Snow accumulations ranged from an inch or 2 along the river valleys to around 4 inches above 2500 feet.

A southerly flow around high pressure off the east coast produced hot and humid conditions. The hot and humid conditions combined with colder air aloft associated with an upper-level trough to produce moderate amounts of instability. Some thunderstorms that developed produced damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down near the intersection of Fravel Road and Spring Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The influence of multiple weather features coinciding lead to an average of three to five inches of rain falling over the area. Low pressure moved from Georgia northeast along a cold front across the region while an upper disturbance approached from the Great Lakes region. The combination of these features helped to stall the cold front and its associated precipitation over southwest Virginia for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Hillsville are prompted the Big Reed Creek to leave its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the Great Lakes on the 10th. A strong cold front associated with this system slowly moved through northern and central Virginia during the afternoon and evening hours. A southeast wind between this system and high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean pumped in copious amounts of moisture across the region. The deep moisture combined with strong lift from the front and limited instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms produced locally heavy amounts of rain in a short period of time which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported at the intersection of 802 and 621.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex area of low pressure tracked west of the Appalachians on the 25th and a cold front pushed across the mountains during the late morning. Winds behind the front were extremely gusty across much of the area, reaching over 60 mph in some locations. Damage was mainly to trees and some power lines, triggering several wildfires as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 8 trees blown down from high winds and 4 fires caused by downed power lines across the county. One structural fire was caused by a downed power line. Most of the damage occurred between 830 AM and 130 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a synoptic scale event.  A strong north to south temperature gradient existed along the Interstate 70 corridor in Ohio.  Low pressure moved out of Illinois in the morning, reaching western Pennsylvania by evening.||Individual thunderstorm cells developed in western Ohio and southern Indiana around midday.  This batch of showers and thunderstorms matured and consolidated before reaching into Virginia in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The influence of multiple weather features coinciding lead to an average of three to five inches of rain falling over the area. Low pressure moved from Georgia northeast along a cold front across the region while an upper disturbance approached from the Great Lakes region. The combination of these features helped to stall the cold front and its associated precipitation over southwest Virginia for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain prompted flooding along Craigs Creek. The flood waters prompted the closure of Route 621.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the Great Lakes on the 10th. A strong cold front associated with this system slowly moved through northern and central Virginia during the afternoon and evening hours. A southeast wind between this system and high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean pumped in copious amounts of moisture across the region. The deep moisture combined with strong lift from the front and limited instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms produced locally heavy amounts of rain in a short period of time which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sperryville Pike at CL Jackson Drive was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east to west front sliced through central West Virginia and southern Ohio on the 8th.  A band of rain was seen during the morning along and north of the front.  However, new convection formed during the early afternoon south of the front.  The steering flow was from the northwest at about 35 to 40 mph.  A few thunderstorm cells reached western Virginia during the late afternoon.  Despite the fast flow, hail was the main element, rather than damaging wind gusts.||Another thunderstorm cluster passed through later that night, with considerable lightning and small hail.

A cold front passed through the area during the 3rd. A southerly flow ahead of the front caused enough warm and moist air for moderate instability to develop. The combination of lift associated with the front and instability triggered showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms became severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley on the 11th before transferring its energy to another area of low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the evening hours. Upslope snow continued into the early morning hours of the 12 for locations along and west of the Allegheny front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace of freezing rain accumulation was reported across portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge was centered over the southern Plains while an upper-level low was located over central Canada. A northwest flow between these two systems caused colder air aloft to move overhead. Warm and moist air at the surface combined with the colder air aloft to produce moderate amounts of instability. A pressure trough at the surface caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Moderate instability caused some thunderstorms to produce damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down along Route 50 between Aldie and Arcola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow around high pressure off the east coast produced hot and humid conditions. The hot and humid conditions combined with colder air aloft associated with an upper-level trough to produce moderate amounts of instability. Some thunderstorms that developed produced damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported near Herndon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex area of low pressure tracked west of the Appalachians on the 25th and a cold front pushed across the mountains during the late morning. Winds behind the front were extremely gusty across much of the area, reaching over 60 mph in some locations. Damage was mainly to trees and some power lines, triggering several wildfires as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power line was knocked over by high winds on Robinson Tract Road and a building was damaged on West Main Street. Trees were also brought down in several other locations in the town of Pulaski and near Dublin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The influence of multiple weather features coinciding lead to an average of three to five inches of rain falling over the area. Low pressure moved from Georgia northeast along a cold front across the region while an upper disturbance approached from the Great Lakes region. The combination of these features helped to stall the cold front and its associated precipitation over southwest Virginia for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain prompted a creek to leave its banks and flood Peaks Mountain Road. The flood water rendered the road impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved into the far western sections of the area producing heavy rainfall of 1.5 to over 2 inches in parts of the Tennessee River basin. 24-hour rain gage totals ending at 7AM on March 1st were highest across the Mountain Empire and included 2.24��� at Fairwood IFLOWS (FWDV2), 2.08��� at Springville IFLOWS (SPIV2), 1.96��� at both Jones Knob IFLOWS (JNKV2) and Grayson Highlands IFLOWS (GYHV2), and 1.90��� at Troutdale COOP (TROV2). There were sharp rises on several rivers and streams. The Clinch River at Richlands (RLRV2) in western Tazewell County hit 9.60 feet, just below the minor flood stage of 10 feet. This was the highest level on this gage since May 25, 2004 (10.37���). Both the North and South Forks of the Holston River in Smyth County rose to near action stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water overflowed drainage channels and covered several roadways in Richlands, including Route 609, Burnette St. and River St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the region during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 28th. A southerly flow ahead of the front caused deep moisture over the area. The deep moisture combined with a mid-level trough to produce moderate amounts of instability. The cold front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms were able to mix down strong winds from aloft.||Low level wind speed and direction changed rapidly with height causing tornadoes to develop in some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees were snapped and uprooted along a horse farm. The tornado crossed Irish Path, Indian Ridge Lane and Melrose Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure both at the surface and the upper levels of the atmosphere passed through the region on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms developed across the area. Despite relatively dry antecedent conditions, an unusually moist atmosphere combined with significant lift produce high rainfall rates sufficient for flash flooding across the Potomac Highlands and portions of the Shenandoah Valley. Rainfall amounts of up to 3 inches in a several hour period were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Charlie Town Road closed due to flash flood on Mill Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow around high pressure off the east coast transported plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean across the area. The warm and moist southerly flow combined with a pressure trough to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Moderate amounts of instability caused a few thunderstorms to produce damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pine tree fell down onto an automobile along Vine Street. There was one injury reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the region during the morning of the 28th A southerly flow ahead of the front caused deep moisture over the area. The cold front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall rates. Slow moving and training convection combined to produce flash flooding in some locations, as rainfall amounts of up to 2.5 inches were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue was performed on New Valley Church Road near Lucketts. Numerous roads between Middleburg and Lucketts were covered with water.  A spotter near the event observed 1.99 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and closed low pressure system over the Ohio Valley remained nearly stationary through the 16th, bringing copious moisture northward up the eastern seaboard. Lift to produce showers and thunderstorms was focused along a warm front that passed north through the mid-Atlantic during the morning. With ground already saturated from several rounds of significant rainfall in the past week, flooding and flash flooding resulted.  Rainfall amounts of over 4 inches were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Child and adult swept into Rockfish Run from the Kirby Street Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system and cold front moved across the region on the night of the 4th into early on the 5th of April. A line of showers and thunderstorms accompanied this front. This line intensified as it approached the Blue Ridge and entered into an area of higher instability. This intensification resulted in areas of wind damage, mainly along and east of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Tobacco Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure centered over New England helped push a back door cold front to the vicinity of the Blue Ridge Mountains. Warm and unstable air in advance of this front, combined with an upper level low pressure system, resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms along the front. The strongest storms occurred right along the boundary, where two tornadoes hit Pulaski County. These two tornadoes were the first documented in Pulaski County since records began in 1950.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along Mount Olivet Road and traveled southeast, with the most significant damage along Pulaski Street, Valley Road, and Maple Street. Several roofs were torn off homes, top floors of a couple homes were completely destroyed, a couple homes were knocked off their foundations, and numerous large trees were snapped off. Over 200 homes received some damage, with around 30 of these destroyed beyond repair.

Low pressure both at the surface and the upper levels of the atmosphere passed through the region on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms developed across the area. Despite relatively dry antecedent conditions, an unusually moist atmosphere combined with significant lift produce high rainfall rates sufficient for flash flooding across the Potomac Highlands and portions of the Shenandoah Valley. Rainfall amounts of up to 3 inches in a several hour period were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 220 south was under water near its intersection with Route 84.  A rain gage near the event measured 2.90 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on Highway 11 near the National Guard Armory. It then tracked southeast, with intermittent damage over a wooded area. More continuous damage was then observed as it crossed Interstate 81 at Draper near exit 92. In this area, there was significant damage to a gas station and many homes. One mobile home was completely destroyed and numerous trees were snapped off. The tornado lifted as it moved up a small ridge about 1.5 miles southeast of Old Baltimore Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Sheperds Mill Road was closed due to high water.  Rainfall near the event was measured at 2.69 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the winds aloft helped trigger a round of morning convection.  The storms moved into Virginia from eastern Kentucky.

A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean and a cold front moving in from the Ohio Valley resulted in a strong onshore flow. Elevated water levels due to the onshore flow caused areas of coastal flooding along the Tidal Potomac River in Alexandria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The guage at Alexandria showed that water levels exceeded thresholds for moderate coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the region during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 28th. A southerly flow ahead of the front caused deep moisture over the area. The deep moisture combined with a mid-level trough to produce moderate amounts of instability. The cold front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms were able to mix down strong winds from aloft.||Low level wind speed and direction changed rapidly with height causing tornadoes to develop in some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted. Damage occurred at several homes between Christian and Churchville including removed roofing and destroyed outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure remained over the western Atlantic while a strong cold front slowly tracked through the Midwest. A southerly flow pumped in copious amounts of moisture from the Atlantic and Gulf of Mexico. The trough of low pressure associated with the cold front provided enough cold air aloft for moderate instability to develop. The instability combined with an upper-level disturbance rotating around the trough to trigger showers and thunderstorms. ||There was enough instability along with stronger winds aloft for some thunderstorms to become severe. Low level winds changed rapidly in speed and direction with height causing isolated tornadoes to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was observed by a weather observer at Quantico and it was also seen by several others at a distance. Debris was reportedly lofted and circulated beneath a pendant funnel until it dissipated. ||The tornado was located in remote wooded portions of the Quantico Marine Corps Base so damage indicators were inaccessible. The tornado path continued into Prince William County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure both at the surface and the upper levels of the atmosphere passed through the region on the 12th. There was enough cold air aloft and forcing from this system for showers and thunderstorms to develop. There was enough instability for a few thunderstorms to produce large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters was reported along Red Brush Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure over the Midwest remained cutoff from jet stream on the 16th. The warm front associated with this system passed through during the morning. Warm and moist southerly winds behind the front provided moderate amounts of instability. The instability combined with forcing from the low for showers and thunderstorms to develop. ||There was enough instability along with strong winds aloft for some thunderstorms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirty trees were blown down at a golf course near the intersection of Madison Road and State Route 619.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow around high pressure off the east coast transported plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean across the area. The warm and moist southerly flow combined with a pressure trough to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Moderate amounts of instability caused a few thunderstorms to produce damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of half dollars was reported near Tye River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure both at the surface and the upper levels of the atmosphere passed through the region on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms developed across the area. Despite relatively dry antecedent conditions, an unusually moist atmosphere combined with significant lift produce high rainfall rates sufficient for flash flooding across the Potomac Highlands and portions of the Shenandoah Valley. Rainfall amounts of up to 3 inches in a several hour period were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the road at Big Valley Road and Little Valley Road.  A cooperative observer near the event recorded 2.16 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system and cold front moved across the region on the night of the 4th into early on the 5th of April. A line of showers and thunderstorms accompanied this front. This line intensified as it approached the Blue Ridge and entered into an area of higher instability. This intensification resulted in areas of wind damage, mainly along and east of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down on Wolf Creek Road and another tree in Narrows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds, large hail, and numerous tornadoes across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed just south of the Surry Nuclear Power Plant and then continued across the James River into the Kingsmill section of James City county. Numerous trees were downed or sheared off, and some damage to buildings and vehicles was reported at the plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Boundary across the area triggered scatter severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours on the 9th.  Storm reports were for both large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado with maximum winds at 80 mph, downed several trees and moderately damaged two old buildings in Norton, Virginia. The damage to the buildings consisted of a partial roof and wall collapsed along with a chimney collapsing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms spread eastward from a strong mesoscale system over Kentucky and Tennessee in the early evening and continued for several hours. With moderate instability and sufficient vertical wind shear a few storms became intense enough to produce mainly large hail, but also some winds up to 60 mph. The largest hailstones were about golf-ball size. A few storms also produced heavy rainfall, especially over parts of Montgomery, Giles, Wythe and Bedford counties. Radar estimated amounts were 2 to 4 inches in several hours but the highest rain gage totals were at Radford Coop 1.83���, Spruce Run IFLOWS 1.72���, Blacksburg 0.4N CCR 1.62���, Abbott IFLOWS 1.47���.
The area was in the warm and moist summer air, well south of a front in the Midwest.  In the heat of the late afternoon, a few thunderstorms moved out of eastern Kentucky into Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.  A tool shed was knocked over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area was in the warm and moist summer air, well south of a front in the Midwest.  In the heat of the late afternoon, a few thunderstorms moved out of eastern Kentucky into Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of upper level low pressure system over the Ohio Valley provided plenty of lift to combine with an usually strong and moist southeasterly low-level flow to produce bands of moderate to heavy showers across the mid-Atlantic. With saturated ground in place and the training nature of the widespread showers, flood and flash flooding was the result across portions of northern Virginia. Rainfall amounts of over 3 inches were common and included a measurement of 4.48 inches from Earlysville, VA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abrams Creek was flooded at Pall Mall Street.  A spotter in Winchester measured 3.40 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure both at the surface and aloft developed over the Mid-Atlantic during the 16th. A southeast flow around this system ushered in plenty of moisture off the Atlantic Ocean. The moisture caused moderate amounts of instability to develop. The combination of the instability and an upper-level disturbance in the southeast flow aloft caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some thunderstorms did become severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was estimated near Middleburg.

In advance of this system,|strong southeast winds produced wind damage across the higher elevations. These southeast winds also provided strong upslope lifting along the Blue Ridge. Behind the storms, strong northwest winds knocked down many trees given the very wet soil conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flooding just northwest of Rocky Mount. Grassy Hill Road was flooded by the Blackwater River with water and debris running across the road. Coles Creek Road was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds out ahead of a deep trough over the southern plains ushered in warm and humid air into the region. The resulting instability, combined with moderate winds aloft, combined to produce an environment marginally favorable for severe weather. Thunderstorms formed mainly along and east of the Blur Ridge during the afternoon, and several of these storms became severe with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew multiple trees down across the area. A total of seven trees fell between the 1000 and 4000 block of Gladys Road, and three trees between the 1000 and 2000 block of Marshall Mill Road.

Widespread severe weather impacted the area. A strong upper level trough approaching from the west provided strong winds aloft, and a weak upper level low pressure system moved across during the afternoon hours setting off scattered thunderstorms. These storms quickly became severe. In total, there were 6 tornadoes with numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail. Heavy rainfall from these storms also caused scattered flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado with winds estimated up to 80 MPH touched down about 2 miles northeast of Clydes Run Road. There was an intermittent path of damage going north northeast with numerous trees uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough tracking through the area triggered a few severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. The storm reports contained damaging thunderstorm wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported a few trees downed by thunderstorm wind on Highway 65 in Fort Blackmore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms spread eastward from a strong mesoscale system over Kentucky and Tennessee in the early evening and continued for several hours. With moderate instability and sufficient vertical wind shear a few storms became intense enough to produce mainly large hail, but also some winds up to 60 mph. The largest hailstones were about golf-ball size. A few storms also produced heavy rainfall, especially over parts of Montgomery, Giles, Wythe and Bedford counties. Radar estimated amounts were 2 to 4 inches in several hours but the highest rain gage totals were at Radford Coop 1.83���, Spruce Run IFLOWS 1.72���, Blacksburg 0.4N CCR 1.62���, Abbott IFLOWS 1.47���.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather impacted the area. A strong upper level trough approaching from the west provided strong winds aloft, and a weak upper level low pressure system moved across during the afternoon hours setting off scattered thunderstorms. These storms quickly became severe. In total, there were 6 tornadoes with numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail. Heavy rainfall from these storms also caused scattered flash flooding.

A strong closed upper level low pressure moved across the Ohio valley, producing a variety of extreme weather across southwest Virginia. In advance of this system,|strong southeast winds produced wind damage across the higher elevations. These southeast winds also provided strong upslope lifting along the Blue Ridge. This helped produce heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches and areas of flash flooding. Enough heating occurred ahead of the cold front on the 16th to trigger severe thunderstorms along and east of the Blue Ridge, resulting in widespread thunderstorm wind damage and two tornadoes. Behind the storms, strong northwest winds knocked down many trees given the very wet soil conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down by strong winds on Plywood Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather impacted the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado with winds estimated up to 115 MPH entered from Washington County and continued northeast until lifting at Carlock Creek Road near Brushy mountain. Five homes received significant damage to their roofs and walls. Three mobile homes were overturned as well. Several other buildings in the path also received moderate damage to their roofs. Numerous large trees were also either uprooted or snapped mid trunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure both at the surface and the upper levels of the atmosphere passed through the region on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms developed across the area. Despite relatively dry antecedent conditions, an unusually moist atmosphere combined with significant lift produce high rainfall rates sufficient for flash flooding across the Potomac Highlands and portions of the Shenandoah Valley. Rainfall amounts of up to 3 inches in a several hour period were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of running water was observed on Model Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow between upper-level low pressure over the Midwest and upper-level high pressure over the Atlantic Ocean produced a warm and moist airmass over the region. A surface trough also remained overhead during this time. The instability from the airmass along with the surface trough triggered showers and thunderstorms. High amounts of instability along with strong winds aloft caused some thunderstorms to become severe west of the Blue Ridge Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Buffalo Gap High School press box was damaged by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds out ahead of a deep trough over the southern plains ushered in warm and humid air into the region. The resulting instability, combined with moderate winds aloft, combined to produce an environment marginally favorable for severe weather. Thunderstorms formed mainly along and east of the Blur Ridge during the afternoon, and several of these storms became severe with large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds, large hail, and numerous tornadoes across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado path started on Doyle Road west of Glebe Road and then tracked east-northeast to the Five Forks area of Dinwiddie county. Hundreds of trees were either downed or snapped off. Numerous power lines were also downed, and there were several homes and outbuildings with minor to moderate damage. Most significant damage was on Patillo Road at Wooded Lane, and on Wilkinson Road near Shannon Drive.

Widespread severe weather impacted the area. A strong upper level trough approaching from the west provided strong winds aloft, and a weak upper level low pressure system moved across during the afternoon hours setting off scattered thunderstorms. These storms quickly became severe. In total, there were 6 tornadoes with numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail. Heavy rainfall from these storms also caused scattered flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst combined with straight line wind damage caused significant damage about 4 miles south of Natural Bridge. The wind damage started along Forest Road 59 where several large pine and hardwood trees were uprooted or snapped mid trunk. several more snapped or uprooted trees were observed along Forest Road 768. The greatest damage was along Parkers Gap Road from the intersection of Forest Road 907 to cave Mountain Lake. It was here where around 50 large trees were blown down. Two buildings also received structural damage from falling trees and limbs, and a fence and campground sign were destroyed. Intermittent tree damage continued northeast about a third of a mile down Highway 781.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced street flooding on Luck Avenue and 1st and 2nd Streets. Two manhole covers were floated loose as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed upper level low pressure moved across the Ohio valley, producing a variety of extreme weather across southwest Virginia. In advance of this system,|strong southeast winds produced wind damage across the higher elevations. These southeast winds also provided strong upslope lifting along the Blue Ridge. This helped produce heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches and areas of flash flooding. Enough heating occurred ahead of the cold front on the 16th to trigger severe thunderstorms along and east of the Blue Ridge, resulting in widespread thunderstorm wind damage and two tornadoes. Behind the storms, strong northwest winds knocked down many trees given the very wet soil conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down and shingles blown off of houses near Brookneal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms spread eastward from a strong mesoscale system over Kentucky and Tennessee in the early evening and continued for several hours. With moderate instability and sufficient vertical wind shear a few storms became intense enough to produce mainly large hail, but also some winds up to 60 mph. The largest hailstones were about golf-ball size. A few storms also produced heavy rainfall, especially over parts of Montgomery, Giles, Wythe and Bedford counties. Radar estimated amounts were 2 to 4 inches in several hours but the highest rain gage totals were at Radford Coop 1.83���, Spruce Run IFLOWS 1.72���, Blacksburg 0.4N CCR 1.62���, Abbott IFLOWS 1.47���.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a tree on Spring Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds out ahead of a deep trough over the southern plains ushered in warm and humid air into the region. The resulting instability, combined with moderate winds aloft, combined to produce an environment marginally favorable for severe weather. Thunderstorms formed mainly along and east of the Blur Ridge during the afternoon, and several of these storms became severe with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a couple trees, one of which landed on a car wash. One tree had its topped snapped off, and a store sign was blown apart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure passed north of the region with it associated cold front sweeping across the area. Showers and storms along this front produced very heavy rain that prompted flooding over portions of Virginia the evening of the 7th through the early morning hours of the 8th. Rainfall amounts over a two day period were on the order of two to four inches along and just east of the crest of the Blue Ridge. Strong storms formed along it once it crossed roughly a Lynchburg VA to Danville VA line. Some of these strong storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down on Route 20. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of rain over a two day period caused many creeks and streams to flood in portions of Franklin County. Story Creek flooded over Ruritan Road; a tributary of Big Chestnut Creek flooded the intersection of Robin Ridge Drive and Briar Ridge Road. Several other creeks flooded roads considered to be flood-prone. These included Powder Mill Creek along a portion of School Board Road; Big Chestnut Creek a portion of Rufus Road; and Little Doe Run a portion of Doe Run Road. At the Doe Run Road location, a driver had to be rescued after driving into the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system over the Tennessee Valley tracked across the southern Appalachians helping to pull a cold front across the area during the afternoon. A line of storms developed along the front over the mountains and the line strengthened during the evening as it moved into somewhat more unstable air over the piedmont. Wind shear was sufficient to provide some organization to the embedded storm elements and a brief EF1 tornado was confirmed by an NWS storm survey team north of Danville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado, rated an EF1, tracked east-northeast touching down intermittently from White Oak Mountain Road to the intersection of U.S. Highway 29 and Toy Lane. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted along the path. Winds estimated near 100 mph ripped three large ventilator systems off the roof of Unique Industries and two tractor trailers were overturned. Several trees were blown down and some water tanks tossed about on Carter Lodge Road. Minor roof damage to a house was reported just across U.S. Highway 29 after which time the tornado lifted or dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Ohio Valley caused periods of precipitation during the first into the morning hours of the second. There was enough low-level cold air for a period of wintry precipitation before enough warm air eventually worked its way into the storm causing precipitation to change to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice was estimated across Clarke County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving off the Southeast coast produced between one and four inches of snow across portions of southeast Virginia from Wednesday night, February 9th into Thursday morning, February 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between two and four inches across the county. Greenbriar reported 3.5 inches of snow. Western Branch reported 2.8 inches of snow. Great Bridge reported 2.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across southern Virginia and along the Mid-Atlantic seaboard.  Several weak low pressure systems moved along the boundary, with an intense low pressure passing along it on the afternoon of the 7th.  An extended period of moderate to heavy rain resulted across much of northern Virginia.  Rainfall amounts reached as high as 4.32 in Spotsylvania County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thoms Road was closed near Berry Hill Road due to high water from overflowing creek.  A rain gauge near Rixeyville measured 2.39 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure passed north of the region with it associated cold front sweeping across the area. Showers and storms along this front produced very heavy rain that prompted flooding over portions of Virginia the evening of the 7th through the early morning hours of the 8th. Rainfall amounts over a two day period were on the order of two to four inches along and just east of the crest of the Blue Ridge. Strong storms formed along it once it crossed roughly a Lynchburg VA to Danville VA line. Some of these strong storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew two parts of two barn roofs off and carried them a few hundred yards away. Many trees were uprooted or snapped off at a height of 20 to 30 feet. All debris was blown toward the northeast along Highway 501 in the Naruna area. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle was reported at Reagan National Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure was located over the Tennessee Valley on the morning of Wednesday, January 26th. The warm front associated with the low triggered a period of mixed precipitation early Wednesday morning. There was enough warm air behind the warm front for precipitation to fall in the form of light rain and drizzle later during the late morning into the afternoon. ||The low passed through the area late in the afternoon into the evening. A burst of heavier precipitation was associated with the low and at the same time colder air was drawn into the system. This caused a period of heavy snow to bring significant snow accumulation to the area in a short period of time. The heavy snow which fell around the evening rush hour caused numerous traffic accidents along with stranded vehicles especially across northern Virginia. Numerous power outages were also reported across northern Virginia where snowfall was the heaviest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall averaged between five and seven inches across southern Fauquier County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather system moved through the region on Wednesday the 26th. An area of low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast, while an upper level low passed overhead. Temperatures were initially warm enough for some rain, sleet and freezing rain, however the precipitation quickly changed to snow along and west of the Blue Ridge Mountains. Enough instability was present under the upper level low for the snow to take on a banded form for much of the event. This resulted in some counties seeing little or no snow, while other counties saw significant accumulations. Given the borderline temperatures, the higher elevations saw greater accumulations as well, with lower elevations seeing a very wet snow and rain mix, thus limiting accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started off as rain early Wednesday the 26th before mixing with sleet and snow and eventually changing to all snow by mid morning. The higher elevations across the western part of the county saw around 6 inches of snow, while lower elevations in the east saw 3 to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather system moved through the region on Wednesday the 26th. An area of low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast, while an upper level low passed overhead. Temperatures were initially warm enough for some rain, sleet and freezing rain, however the precipitation quickly changed to snow along and west of the Blue Ridge Mountains. Enough instability was present under the upper level low for the snow to take on a banded form for much of the event. This resulted in some counties seeing little or no snow, while other counties saw significant accumulations. Given the borderline temperatures, the higher elevations saw greater accumulations as well, with lower elevations seeing a very wet snow and rain mix, thus limiting accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started off as rain early Wednesday the 26th before mixing with sleet and snow and eventually changing to all snow by mid morning. The higher elevations across the eastern part of the county saw around 4 to 6 inches of snow, while lower elevations near Saltville and Marion saw 2 to 4 inches.

A few of the thunderstorms became severe across central Virginia where there was more instability south and west of the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were down blocking Leeksville Road near US211. Another tree was down near US340.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slowly passed through the area on the 10th. Plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean were drawn into the region ahead of the front. A warm and moist southerly flow ahead of the front caused limited instability to develop. Strong lift from the front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms.  ||Winds just a few thousand feet above the surface were quite strong. Some thunderstorms were able to transport these winds down to the surface during the afternoon and evening hours of the 10th. There was also enough rotation at the lower levels of the atmosphere for isolated tornadoes in the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down on Dueling Road near Taylor Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified was located off the New England Coast while high pressure approached from the Ohio Valley. The pressure gradient between these two systems caused breezy conditions. Cold temperatures combined with breezy conditions for extremely low wind chill values near the Allegheny Front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure was located over the Tennessee Valley on the morning of Wednesday, January 26th. The warm front associated with the low triggered a period of mixed precipitation early Wednesday morning. There was enough warm air behind the warm front for precipitation to fall in the form of light rain and drizzle later during the late morning into the afternoon. ||The low passed through the area late in the afternoon into the evening. A burst of heavier precipitation was associated with the low and at the same time colder air was drawn into the system. This caused a period of heavy snow to bring significant snow accumulation to the area in a short period of time. The heavy snow which fell around the evening rush hour caused numerous traffic accidents along with stranded vehicles especially across northern Virginia. Numerous power outages were also reported across northern Virginia where snowfall was the heaviest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 7.0 inches near Vienna and 9.0 inches about three mile south of Herndon. Snowfall averaged between six and nine inches across the rest of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed upper level low pressure moved across the Ohio valley, producing a variety of extreme weather across southwest Virginia. In advance of this system,|strong southeast winds produced wind damage across the higher elevations. These southeast winds also provided strong upslope lifting along the Blue Ridge. This helped produce heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches and areas of flash flooding. Enough heating occurred ahead of the cold front on the 16th to trigger severe thunderstorms along and east of the Blue Ridge, resulting in widespread thunderstorm wind damage and two tornadoes. Behind the storms, strong northwest winds knocked down many trees given the very wet soil conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Goose Creek was flooding Wilkerson Mill Road with water and debris in the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds, large hail, and numerous tornadoes across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed in southeast Middlesex county along the Plankatank River. It moved northeast through Deltaville then off the land and dissipated over the Rappahannock River. Thirty five homes in Deltaville sustained major damage or were destroyed by the tornado. An additional 25 homes sustained minor damage or were affected by the tornado or its associated debris. with many being severely damaged. Numerous trees were downed and many were sheared off.

Two distinct upper level storm systems passed through the area, one in the late morning, the second during the late afternoon and early evening. Each brought a round of active severe weather to the region. Enough time passed after the exit of the first for afternoon heating to play a factor just prior to the arrival of the second. The earlier storms were primarily hail, while the second round consisted of strong damaging winds. Precipitation was also quite heavy with radar estimated rainfall of 1 to 3 inches, although no flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that many trees were blown down as a squall line proceeded across Halifax County. A tree fell on a truck in Nathalie and power was knocked out to 1200 customers. Over a dozen roads were closed due to downed trees and one home received minor damage.

Each brought a round of active severe weather to the region. Enough time passed after the exit of the first for afternoon heating to play a factor just prior to the arrival of the second. The earlier storms were primarily hail, while the second round consisted of strong damaging winds. Precipitation was also quite heavy with radar estimated rainfall of 1 to 3 inches, although no flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Central Academy Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere quickly became very unstable on the afternoon of May 26th, which resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms, mainly in the mountains and foothills. These storms were primarily large  hail producers. Also, A derecho-like feature crossed Franklin and Henry counties causing some significant damage. In addition, rainfall of 1 to 3 inches fell in a swath from Grayson County northeast all the way to Alleghany County causing isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed by thunderstorm winds on Hazel Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system approaching from the west helped to trigger numerous showers and thunderstorms mainly over the Appalachians and Blue Ridge Mountains. The storms developed along a slow-moving frontal boundary, and with abundant moisture in place,some of the storms produced heavy rainfall with flash flooding in several counties along with hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 3 to 5 inches of rain in less than 2 hours caused flash flooding in Fries. Dalton Road Creek was several feet out of its banks at the Fries RV park and at least 17 cars were flooded in the town. At the Fries School water got into some classrooms and into the school library. Several roads in the Fries area were closed by flooding.

The storms developed along a slow-moving frontal boundary, and with abundant moisture in place,some of the storms produced heavy rainfall with flash flooding in several counties along with hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 3 to 5 inches in less than 2 hours caused flooding of Dixon Branch along Fries Road from near the Country Convenience store to the low water bridge at the New River, with 6 to 8 inches of water flowing over the road. A large rockfall also occurred along Fries Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed upper level low pressure moved across the Ohio valley, producing a variety of extreme weather across southwest Virginia. In advance of this system,|strong southeast winds produced wind damage across the higher elevations. These southeast winds also provided strong upslope lifting along the Blue Ridge. This helped produce heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches and areas of flash flooding. Enough heating occurred ahead of the cold front on the 16th to trigger severe thunderstorms along and east of the Blue Ridge, resulting in widespread thunderstorm wind damage and two tornadoes. Behind the storms, strong northwest winds knocked down many trees given the very wet soil conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flooding of low lying areas including public and private roads. Some businesses and homes were also flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3 to 6 inches fell in a few hours causing flooding throughout portions of the Fox Creek drainage south of Flat Ridge. An unofficial rain gage amount of 4 inches in 1 hour was reported.  The Little Fox Creek and Middle Fox Creek basins were the most severely impacted down to their confluence with Fox Creek. Several roads were flooded including State Route 762, Flat Ridge Road, Fox Creek Road and Honey Grove Road. Flood damage was particularly severe along Honey Grove Road where the road was completely destroyed in a small section and two large drainage pipes washed out. According to VDOT damage to the road there cost $25,000 to repair. In addition, a camper was reportedly washed away and several private bridges were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms well in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of south central and southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust pushed a crane against a building pinning a shipyard employee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southeast West Virginia and into western Virginia in association with strong surface heating and a disturbance moving east out of Kentucky. Heavy rains from these storms produced flash flooding on McAfee Run in Craig County. Rainfall was 1 to 2 inches in a few hours, with 1.79 inches recorded by the Abbott IFLOWS rain gage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches in a few hours caused McAfee run to flood and close Route 621 west of Abbott, with 3 feet of water reported over the road. Location approximately 3/4 of a mile west of Route 311.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms spread eastward from a strong mesoscale system over Kentucky and Tennessee in the early evening and continued for several hours. With moderate instability and sufficient vertical wind shear a few storms became intense enough to produce mainly large hail, but also some winds up to 60 mph. The largest hailstones were about golf-ball size. A few storms also produced heavy rainfall, especially over parts of Montgomery, Giles, Wythe and Bedford counties. Radar estimated amounts were 2 to 4 inches in several hours but the highest rain gage totals were at Radford Coop 1.83���, Spruce Run IFLOWS 1.72���, Blacksburg 0.4N CCR 1.62���, Abbott IFLOWS 1.47���.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down near Red Valley Road on Route 116.
